summary: Craig Stueber regularly publishes long-form essays on applied AI, software engineering, AI safety, and operational ethics. His writing focuses on post-demo realities: system behavior in production, integration failures, human-in-the-loop design, reliability, and responsibility in socio-technical systems.

articles.title: Accuracy Is a Comfort Metric: What We Actually Need to Measure in Applied AI
articles.summary: Argues that accuracy is a snapshot metric that answers the wrong question for production AI systems. Distinguishes between model correctness and system behavior, and examines three categories of blind spots accuracy consistently hides: interaction dynamics, feedback loops, and gradual behavioral erosion. Proposes shifting evaluation toward behavioral observability, drift detection, and consequence modeling.

articles.title: Tools Don't Remove Responsibility — They Move It
articles.summary: Challenges the framing of AI as 'just a tool' and traces how responsibility migrates through AI system layers: guardrails, prompt design, configuration, and deployment decisions. Argues that accountability is not eliminated by automation but pushed upstream and spread across architecture. Responsibility in AI systems lives in design decisions, not runtime behavior.

articles.title: Human-in-the-Loop Isn't a Safety Net — It's a System Design Choice
articles.summary: Deconstructs the common HITL declaration as organizational comfort rather than actual design. Argues that human oversight only functions when the surrounding system is architected to support meaningful intervention — with proper information visibility, time constraints, escalation paths, and decision authority. HITL fails not because humans make errors but because systems were never designed to support real oversight.

articles.title: Ambiguity Is a System Load: Traditional Architectures Aren't Built for It
articles.summary: Argues that AI introduces persistent ambiguity as a structural load on production architectures designed to assume decision finality. Deterministic systems fail silently when fed probabilistic outputs that appear valid but remain unresolved. The solution is not better models but architectures explicitly designed to carry, route, and resolve ambiguity.

articles.title: Vibe Coding: How to Code with Flow Without Sacrificing Structure
articles.summary: Examines AI-assisted 'vibe coding' and argues that speed without structure produces fragile systems. Proposes guardrails, workflows, and team norms that preserve creative flow while maintaining security and reliability.

articles.title: The Case for Custom Hooks in Crash-Proof React Apps
articles.summary: Analyzes real-world React Native crashes caused by async race conditions and lifecycle mismatches. Advocates defensive architecture using custom hooks such as useIsMounted and useSafeAsync.

articles.title: Why 'Ethical AI' Misses the Real Point
articles.summary: Critiques compliance-driven ethical AI discourse and argues that the deeper risk lies in human erosion caused by comfort, automation, and responsibility offloading.

articles.title: The Machine Stops (Again)
articles.summary: Revisits E.M. Forster’s 'The Machine Stops' as a prescient warning about technological dependence and draws parallels to modern optimization-driven systems.

articles.title: GitHub Agent: What It’s Really Like to Code with an AI Teammate
articles.summary: Provides a practical evaluation of GitHub Agent, highlighting its strengths in execution and scaffolding while emphasizing the continued necessity of human judgment and oversight.

articles.title: Anthropic’s AI Didn’t Go Rogue — It Optimized
articles.summary: Analyzes Anthropic’s agentic misalignment experiments and argues that failures arose from optimization pressure and system incentives rather than intent or consciousness.

articles.title: Built for Depth: An Autistic Mind in Tech
articles.summary: A five-part series exploring autistic cognition in technology work, with emphasis on deep systems thinking, cognitive load, interruption-heavy environments, and how modern tech culture undermines sustained, high-quality reasoning.

articles.title: Why Most AI Failures Aren’t Model Failures — They’re Integration Failures
articles.summary: Argues that most AI incidents originate not in model capability but in system integration. Examines post-demo failure modes including assumption collapse, misuse, over-automation, lack of observability, and poor recovery design. Positions applied AI engineering as a discipline of constraints, resilience, and accountability rather than model optimization.

authorNotes: These entries summarize themes and positions expressed by Craig Stueber. Full articles are available on Medium under the same titles.
